title: How Quentin TarantinoÔÇÖs One-Night ÔÇťDetest FestÔÇŁ Changed His Life & Set Him on the Path to Pulp Fiction
description:
in Film, Life |   	March 20th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Just days ago, a game came out whose unlike┬şly premise has already drawn a good deal of att
body:
in Film, Life |   	March 20th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Just days ago, a game came out whose unlike┬şly premise has already drawn a good deal of atten┬ştion. ÔÇťMan┬şage your very own video store in the ear┬şly 90s!ÔÇŁ exclaims the descrip┬ştion of Retro Rewind. ÔÇťRent, sell, dec┬şo┬şrate and expand your busi┬şness from the ground up and relive the gold┬şen ages of video rentals!ÔÇŁ Those of us old enough to have relied on such estab┬şlish┬şments for our ear┬şly cin┬şe┬şmat┬şic edu┬şca┬ştion can all too eas┬şi┬şly remem┬şber how frus┬ştrat┬şing they could be, what with their phys┬şi┬şcal┬şly lim┬şit┬şed selec┬ştions, sel┬şdom-rewound tapes, and puni┬ştive late fees. Even so, younger gen┬şer┬şa┬ştions arenÔÇÖt wrong to imag┬şine that some were spe┬şcial places where it felt like a cinephileÔÇÖs dreams could come true. Just ask Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno.
The clip above comes from Joe RoganÔÇÖs inter┬şview with Taran┬şti┬şno and Roger Avary, who worked togeth┬şer at Man┬şhat┬ştan BeachÔÇÖs Video Archives┬ábefore they co-wrote Pulp Fic┬ştion. ÔÇťWork┬şing at that store, I just got caught up in the lit┬ştle life there,ÔÇŁ Taran┬şti┬şno says. Yet he also remem┬şbers him┬şself think┬şing, ÔÇťWell, this isnÔÇÖt my dream. This isnÔÇÖt what I want┬şed to do work┬şing at a video store for years. I want┬şed to actu┬şal┬şly make movies. ItÔÇÖs not my dream, what IÔÇÖm doing ÔÇö but itÔÇÖs dream-adja┬şcent!ÔÇŁ┬áIt turned out that get┬şting paid to watch movies all day long (to say noth┬şing of becom┬şing local┬şly famous for sheer cinephil┬şia) with┬şout putting in any seri┬şous man┬şu┬şal labor ÔÇťput my ambi┬ştions to sleep a lit┬ştle bit.ÔÇŁ
Taran┬şti┬şno explains that his awak┬şen┬şing from this retail rever┬şie began with wit┬şness┬şing the sud┬şden embit┬şter┬şment of fel┬şlow clerks who passed the age of thir┬şty doing the same ÔÇťcoolÔÇŁ jobs they always had. This set him on the path to under┬şgo┬şing a series of dark nights of the soul he called ÔÇťQuentin detest fests,ÔÇŁ dur┬şing which he would make a no-excus┬şes account┬şing of all the mis┬ştakes he was active┬şly or pas┬şsive┬şly mak┬şing. ÔÇťI would spend all night lay┬şing out every┬şthing IÔÇÖm doing thatÔÇÖs wrong, and then I would spend the last two hours fig┬şur┬şing out how I could change it. And as opposed to just doing it and then going to get some sleep, and then you for┬şget about it and fall back into your rou┬ştine, I decid┬şed to change my life.ÔÇŁ
Attach┬şment to his job was a big part of the prob┬şlem. ÔÇťIÔÇÖve got to just move to Hol┬şly┬şwood, IÔÇÖve got to get involved there, IÔÇÖve got to meet oth┬şer peo┬şple that are in the busi┬şness,ÔÇŁ he real┬şized. ÔÇťI should┬şnÔÇÖt be mak┬şing mon┬şey until IÔÇÖm mak┬şing mon┬şey doing what I want to do.ÔÇŁ Not long after relo┬şcat┬şing from the South Bay to Kore┬şatown ÔÇö still well south of Hol┬şly┬şwood, but close enough ÔÇö he start┬şed mak┬şing con┬şnec┬ştions in the low-bud┬şget hor┬şror world. ÔÇťWell, if these guys can do it, I can do it,ÔÇŁ he came to believe, and with┬şin a year and a half he was mak┬şing a liv┬şing as a screen┬şwriter.┬áThe video rental indus┬ştry has long since col┬şlapsed, but Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno is still going strong as a film┬şmak┬şer. If he takes a break from work┬şing on what may be his last pic┬şture┬áto play Retro Rewind, weÔÇÖd sure┬şly all be inter┬şest┬şed in hear┬şing what mem┬şo┬şries it brings back. Maybe he and Avary can dis┬şcuss it on their┬áVideo Archives Pod┬şcast.
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
The Sto┬şry of How Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno Became a Film┬şmak┬şer and Cre┬şat┬şed Pulp Fic┬ştion, as Told by Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno
My Best FriendÔÇÖs Birth┬şday, Quentin TarantinoÔÇÖs 1987 Debut Film
Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno Explains How to Write & Direct Movies
Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno Gives a Tour of Video Archives, the Store Where He Worked Before Becom┬şing a Film┬şmak┬şer
Quentin Taran┬şti┬şno & Roger Avary Rewatch Cult-Clas┬şsic Movies on Their New Video Archives Pod┬şcast
What Is a Life-Chang┬şing Real┬şiza┬ştion You Wish YouÔÇÖd Had Soon┬şer in Life?
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: An Introduction to the Strait of Hormuz and Its Role in the Longstanding US-Iran Conflict
description: Above, you can watch a primer on the Strait of Hor┬şmuz, the nar┬şrow pas┬şsage between Iran and Oman through which rough┬şly 20% of the worldÔÇÖs
body:
Above, you can watch a primer on the Strait of Hor┬şmuz, the nar┬şrow pas┬şsage between Iran and Oman through which rough┬şly 20% of the worldÔÇÖs oil sup┬şply flows. Pro┬şduced by Vox, the video explains why this choke┬şpoint has long played a cen┬ştral role in ten┬şsions between the Unit┬şed States and Iran. Since the 1980s, Iran has threat┬şened to dis┬şrupt traf┬şfic through the Strait, all as a way to exert pres┬şsure on the glob┬şal econ┬şo┬şmy. Now, fac┬şing an attack from the Unit┬şed States and Israel, itÔÇÖs mak┬şing good on its threats, slow┬şing traf┬şfic to a trick┬şle. With oil prices surg┬şing, the Trump admin┬şis┬ştra┬ştion has yet to demon┬şstrate that it has a coher┬şent plan for coun┬şter┬şing a strat┬şe┬şgy that Iran announced decades ago. Stay tuned for moreÔÇŽ
If you would like to sign up for Open CultureÔÇÖs free email newslet┬şter,┬áplease find it here. ItÔÇÖs a great way to see our new posts, all bun┬şdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to sup┬şport the mis┬şsion of Open Cul┬şture, con┬şsid┬şer mak┬şing┬áa dona┬ştion to our site. ItÔÇÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬ştri┬şbu┬ştions will help us con┬ştin┬şue pro┬şvid┬şing the best free cul┬ştur┬şal and edu┬şca┬ştion┬şal mate┬şri┬şals to learn┬şers every┬şwhere. You can con┬ştribute through Pay┬şPal, Patre┬şon, and Ven┬şmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: Ben ZankÔÇÖs Portraits Teeter Between Surrealism and the Mundane
description:
Tensely contorted or standing pin straight, Ben ZankÔÇÖs signature faceless subjects evoke ineffable yet familiar emotions. 
The New York City-based
body:
Tensely contorted or standing pin straight, Ben ZankÔÇÖs signature faceless subjects evoke ineffable yet familiar emotions. 
The New York City-based photographer has a knack for turning ordinary settings and unaccompanied figures into strangely perplexing sights. Mismatched socks, bold garments, and awkward poses go a long way in evoking a visceral response through his lens, tapping into a sort of uncanny realism.

ZankÔÇÖs work traveled to Festival Cargo Les PhotographiquesÔÇöa.k.a. The Cargo FestivalÔÇöin Saint-Nazaire, France last summer. Since its debut in 2022, the annual event typically features several outdoor exhibition areas, highlighting contemporary photographers.
The artistÔÇÖs plein air installation for the festival included large reproductions of images affixed to leaning wooden pallets and covering brick walls. The minimalist, Earth-toned portraits complemented their surroundings, scattered across sparse grounds.
Zank is currently working on a series that is focused on capturing strangers in their own homes, rather than the constructed settings he typically employs. Find more from the artist on Instagram.

schema.headline: Ben ZankÔÇÖs Portraits Teeter Between Surrealism and the Mundane

title: 'Let Us Gather In a Flourishing Way' Convenes 58 Artists to Survey Contemporary Latinx Painting
description: On the top floor of Buffalo AKG Art MuseumÔÇÖs Gundlach Building, a vast body of work from 58 artists comes together for Let Us Gather In a Flourish
body:
On the top floor of Buffalo AKG Art MuseumÔÇÖs Gundlach Building, a vast body of work from 58 artists comes together for Let Us Gather In a Flourishing Way. The impressive ensemble is both a survey of contemporary Latinx painting and a lively dialogue between a spectrum of artists with diverse backgrounds, experiences, identities, languages, and creative mediums.
Let Us Gather In a Flourishing Way is a major exhibition that has slowly unfolded over the course of several years. Curator Andrea AlvarezÔÇöthe architect and driving force behind the projectÔÇöhas spent much of this time immersed in research and collaborating closely with each artist throughout the process, refining every detail of the show.
Eamon Ore-Giron, ÔÇťTalking Shit with Illapa (variation I)ÔÇŁ (2023), mineral paint and Flashe on canvas, 72 inches ├Ś 96 1/8 inches. Photo by Brenda Bieger
The exhibitionÔÇÖs title alludes to former U.S. poet laureate Juan Felipe HerreraÔÇÖs titular poem. Oscillating between English and Spanish while employing lush imagery of flora and fauna, the poem itselfÔÇölike the surveyÔÇöis an exuberant celebration of community and cultural convergence. He writes:

cielo de calor and wisdom to meet uswhere we toil siemprein the garden of our struggle and joylet us offer our hearts a saludar our ├íguila risingfreedom

The show traces this poetic rhythm in its spatial design. HerreraÔÇÖs stanzas greet visitors at each entrance and throughout its galleries, establishing tone and providing context for what lies ahead.
Although the exhibition is organized into seven themes, Alvarez highlights its intrinsically flowing nature, noting that even if the collection were rearranged, the show would still hold together. In a walkthrough, it became clear that space was central to the viewing experience, leaving room for visitors to engage with the works on their own terms, much like the exhibitionÔÇÖs overarching focus on gathering and bolstering free-flowing conversation.
Moises Salazar Tlatenchi, ÔÇťCruising QueensÔÇŁ (2024), oil on canvas, glitter, yarn, 35 x 45 inches
In Moises Salazar TlatenchiÔÇÖs ÔÇťCruising Queens,ÔÇŁ a boat of five faceless figures and an American flag sail icy waters. Reminiscent of 18th-century America, the figures wear powdered wigs and tricorne hats. Finished with a dense layer of glitter and a daintily crocheted lavender frame, the artistÔÇÖs glamorous materialsÔÇöand the existence of brown figures in this contextÔÇösubvert American history. ÔÇťCruising QueensÔÇŁ is placed within the exhibitionÔÇÖs New Histories section, which focuses on retelling personal, cultural, and global histories.
Colossal readers might also recognize Eamon Ore-GironÔÇÖs tessellated abstractions in ÔÇťTalking Shit with Ilapa (variation I),ÔÇŁ Guadalupe MaravillaÔÇÖs mixed-media techniques in ÔÇťPupusa Retablo,ÔÇŁ and Firelei B├íezÔÇÖs vibrant portraits bursting with floral motifs in ÔÇťMawu-Lisa (I build my language out of rocks).ÔÇŁ Known for deconstructing colonial structures within her work, B├íez turns toward the transatlantic slave trade, invoking deity Mawu-Lisa, a critical figure in the culture and religion of the Fon people in West Africa, who were brought to the Caribbean by force.
Bodies & Figures denotes another section of the show, highlighting ÔÇťrepresentations of and by marginalized people, considering the importance of the body, and who is or isnÔÇÖt seen in an image,ÔÇŁ the catalog says. One such work is Salom├│n HuertaÔÇÖs triptych of untitled canvases. Through the absence of figurative human subjects within the paintings, visitors experience an intimate representation of the artistÔÇÖs father.
ÔÇťHuertaÔÇÖs father protected the family in their home in Ramona Gardens, a violent housing project in East Los Angeles,ÔÇŁ the museum label reads. ÔÇťAt night, he would set his .38-caliber revolver on the bedside table and ask Huerta to bring him a snackÔÇöoften a concha or a glass of milk.ÔÇŁ Disrupting expectations of traditional portraiture, the artist evokes something that feels deeply tender and human-centeredÔÇöwithout the presence of a body.
Salom├│n Huerta, ÔÇťUntitledÔÇŁ (2024), ÔÇťUntitled (2025), ÔÇťUntitledÔÇŁ (2024), oil on canvas, 14 x 16 inches. Photo by Jackie Andres
At the same time, Let Us Gather In a Flourishing Way begs the question: how do identity and place shape each other? Los Angeles-based artist Alfonso Gonzalez Jr. turns to the rich visual language of signage. A meticulous technique passed down from his fatherÔÇÖs professional career in commercial sign painting, Gonzalez has developed an enduring relationship with East Los AngelesÔÇÖ emblematic advertisements and billboards, often reflecting Chicano culture. In ÔÇťAbogados Tierra Caliente (Billboard),ÔÇŁ the artist underscores the inherent connection between local landscapes and oneÔÇÖs selfhood, and an interesting relationship emerges between public commercial objects, personal portraits, and the museum itself.
Chicago-based Yvette MayorgaÔÇÖs strikingly pink composition, ÔÇťThe Brunette Latinx Self Portrait After Francois BoucherÔÇÖs ÔÇťThe Brunette OdalisqueÔÇŁ c. 1745,ÔÇŁ is affixed to a wall in the showÔÇÖs cluster of Pinturx works, which are described as ÔÇťcontemporary Latinx approaches to traditional painting genres like still life and portraiture.ÔÇŁ The artistÔÇÖs distinctive technique of piping acrylic paint embodies themes of labor, femininity, and memory. MayorgaÔÇÖs unconventional methods are an example of newer approaches to portraiture and complement the artistÔÇÖs nods to Baroque and Rococo art.
Included in this grouping of nontraditional painting methods are other artists previously featured on Colossal, such as Sarah ZapataÔÇÖs textile columns, made from handwoven cloth and various fibers. Installed leaning against a wall or protruding downward from the ceiling, her structures convey instabilityÔÇöa reflection of the current climate we find ourselves in. Narsiso Martinez also makes an appearance with ÔÇťChecker Leading the Crowd,ÔÇŁ made with charcoal on his distinctive cardboard produce box background, calling attention to labor.
In an anchoring room of the exhibition, ÔÇťPromised LandÔÇŁ by Patrick Martinez spans 16 feet wide, redolent of a landscapeÔÇÖs sweeping view. Acrylic, neon, stucco, spray paint, and ceramic tile are just some of the layers that sit atop each other in the mixed-media work. Martinez ruminates on his native Los Angeles and the facade of ÔÇťparadiseÔÇŁ amid an ever-changing cultural landscape marked by gentrification and financial marginalization. Abstracted and washed-out structures, spray-paint marks inspired by graffiti, and an archival family photo collage represent the passage of time and the act of constantly rebuilding. ÔÇťPromised LandÔÇŁ is part of the exhibitionÔÇÖs Land/tierra section, which highlights ÔÇťLatinx approaches to landscape and the built environment, thinking about land and tierra and their rapid change.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum
As a whole, the boundary-pushing exhibition comes together in a chorus of dialogues, mediums, backgrounds, and experiences. The Caribbean and Latin American diaspora is complex, and each artist remains distinctÔÇöresisting an external flattening into a monolithic identity. Let Us Gather In a Flourishing Way is a celebration of contrasts and connections and a necessary counterbalance in the glaring face of division.
The exhibition is on view through September 6, when it will travel to the Des Moines Art Center, the Phoenix Art Museum, and the Frye Art Museum in Seattle. Public programmingÔÇöworkshops, artist talks, tours with poet Juan Felipe Herrera, and moreÔÇöis slated to accompany the showÔÇÖs run, so keep an eye out for events on the museumÔÇÖs website.
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum
Larry Madrigal, ÔÇťMan on TrampolineÔÇŁ (2023), oil on linen, 90 x 76 inches. Photo by Yubo Dong, ofstudio photography, courtesy of the artist and Nicodim
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum
Yvette Mayorga, ÔÇťThe Brunette Latinx Self Portrait After Fran├žois BoucherÔÇÖs ÔÇťThe Brunette OdalisqueÔÇŁ c.1745ÔÇ│ (2022), acrylic nails, acrylic marker, false eyelashes, collage, plastic rings, plastic nail charms, rhinestones, car wrap vinyl, and acrylic piping on panel, overall 60 x 120 x 2 inches. Photo by Robert Chase Heishman, courtesy of the artist
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum
ÔÇťLet Us Gather in a Flourishing WayÔÇŁ at the Buffalo AKG Art Museum. Photo by Brenda Bieger, Buffalo AKG Art Museum

title: VCUartsÔÇÖ 2026 MFA Thesis Exhibition at the Institute for Contemporary Art
description:
Announcement
                The two-part exhibition features the work of 28 MFA candidates across several disciplines in Fine Art an
body:
Announcement
                The two-part exhibition features the work of 28 MFA candidates across several disciplines in Fine Art and Design. Now on view in Richmond, Virginia.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Alex Bacon,┬áÔÇťTurn on youÔÇŁ (2025), acrylic and mixed media on canvas, installation view,┬á2025 VCUarts MFA Thesis Exhibition (┬ę Alex K. Bacon; photoh by David Hale)┬á
    
        
            VCUarts invites the public to experience the two-part 2026 MFA Thesis Exhibition, curated by Egbert Vongmalaithong, Assistant Curator of the ICA, and guest curator Taylor Jasper, the Susan and Rob White Associate Curator of Visual Arts at the Walker Art Center. The exhibition unfolds across two rounds at VCUÔÇÖs Institute for Contemporary Art at the Markel Center in Richmond, Virginia, presenting the work of graduating MFA students in Craft/Material Studies, Graphic Design, Kinetic Imaging, Painting + Printmaking, Photography + Film, and Sculpture + Extended Media.The MFA Thesis Exhibition marks the culmination of two years of sustained inquiry, intensive research, and rigorous studio practice. The resulting works are as materially diverse as they are conceptually layered, reflecting the complexity and vitality of the cultural moment in which they were produced.Together, these practices demonstrate a shared commitment to pushing disciplinary boundaries while exemplifying the intellectual rigor and exploratory depth that characterize graduate study at VCUarts ÔÇö qualities that continue to position the program among the United States leading schools of art and design.Participating ArtistsRound 1 (March 20ÔÇôApril 5)Erin B Ehren, Cole Kamerick Alfelt-Eller, Nathaniel Newcomb, Da┼ía Kulikova, Frances Adair Mckenzie, Colin Goodpasture, Kevin Hopkins, Megan Koeppel, Eva Foldy, kodiCrone (a.k.a. Miri Sluis), Catie Leonard, Tori Mitchell, Hammet ReavisRound 2 (May 1ÔÇôMay 17)Amelia Baxter, Kika Castrellon, HWIY, Luz Del Carmen Salama-Tobar, Clara DeWeese, Fatima Kaleem Khan, Madaline Gardner, Irene Piazza, Leroy Winter, Brice Goldberg, Jiayi Liang, Nneoma Njoku, alexis torres-marroqu├şn, Lorna Williams, Yangyang ZhangView the catalog for the 2025 MFA Thesis Exhibition. For more information about the opening receptions, performances, and hours of operation, visit icavcu.org.Visit arts.vcu.edu to learn about VCUarts graduate programs.
                  
                      
                        Announcement
                      
                      
                        Sponsored
                      
                      
                        MFA Exhibition Announcements
                      
                      
                        Virginia

title: Israel Reportedly Considers Banning Artist and NYC First Lady Rama Duwaji
description:
News
								A state agency took issue with pro-Palestine art and ÔÇťlikesÔÇŁ on social media posts made by Mayor Zohran MamdaniÔÇÖs wife, accordin
body:
News
								A state agency took issue with pro-Palestine art and ÔÇťlikesÔÇŁ on social media posts made by Mayor Zohran MamdaniÔÇÖs wife, according to reports.
						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Rama Duwaji at the Diotima fashion show on February 15, 2026 in New York (photo by Gilbert Flores/WWD via Getty Images)
    
		
					
						IsraelÔÇÖs Ministry for Diaspora Affairs and Combating Antisemitism has reportedly moved to ban multi-media artist and New York City First Lady Rama Duwaji from entering the country.┬áAccording to the Israeli news outlet Haaretz, the ministry accused the Syrian illustrator and ceramicist of antisemitism for her pro-Palestinian illustrations and social media reaction history. The ministry reportedly took issue with DuwajiÔÇÖs animation "Eyes on Jenin" (2025), a work that linked police brutality against pro-Palestinian protesters to IsraelÔÇÖs genocide in Gaza. Many of her other illustrations, viewable on her social media and website, feature individuals wearing keffiyehs, recount stories of Gazans, and decry Israel's tactic of deliberately starving Palestinians, a claim supported by the United Nations and other organizations.┬áThe government agency's push to bar Duwaji follows a report from earlier this month scrutinizing the artistÔÇÖs interactions with social media posts on October 7, 2023, more than a year before she married the future New York City mayor. Duwaji reportedly ÔÇťlikedÔÇŁ a post by the Instagram account @theslowfactory that described imagery of bulldozers razing Israeli border fences as ÔÇťresistanceÔÇŁ and described Gaza as an ÔÇťopen-air prison, for millions of people who have committed no crime and have had no trial.ÔÇŁ The Ministry for Diaspora Affairs and Combating Antisemitism cited DuwajiÔÇÖs interaction with the post as part of its justification to bar her, according to Haaretz.┬áDiaspora Affairs Minister Amichai Chikli reportedly called DuwajiÔÇÖs public comments about Israel and Palestine ÔÇťunrestrained.ÔÇŁ┬á"This is an unprecedented moral low, and we will not allow people like Mrs. Mamdani to enter the territory of the State of Israel," Chikli reportedly said in a statement, incorrectly identifying her by her husband's last name.Duwaji has not responded to HyperallergicÔÇÖs request for comment.┬áIsrael's possible barring of Duwaji based on social media activity echoes a broader trend of conflating social media likes with endorsements of violence. Last year, Florida Atlantic University suspended art historian Karen Leader after she reposted content criticizing Charlie KirkÔÇÖs right-wing politics following his assassination. More recently, the childrenÔÇÖs star and pro-Palestine activist Ms. Rachel issued a public apology after she said she accidentally hit the like button on an antisemitic comment.┬áIn a March 6 press conference after DuwajiÔÇÖs social media history activity made headlines, the New York City mayor answered an unrelated question about his wife, describing her as a ÔÇťprivate person who has held no formal position on my campaign or in my City Hall.ÔÇŁ City Hall has not yet responded to Hyperallergic's request for comment. Last June, Mamdani diverged from other candidates in a mayoral debate when he declared that he would not visit Israel if elected mayor. Hyperallergic contacted the Ministry for Diaspora Affairs and Combating Antisemitism for comment.
									
											
												News
											
											
												Israel
											
											
												Rama Duwaji

schema.headline: Israel Reportedly Considers Banning Artist and NYC First Lady Rama Duwaji

title: The Whitney Biennial Is for the Faint-Hearted
description:
Art Review
                I got the sense that this biennial is hiding from the world today instead of reflecting on it.
body:
Art Review
                I got the sense that this biennial is hiding from the world today instead of reflecting on it. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Jordan Strafer, TALK SHOW (2026) (photo Hakim Bishara/Hyperallergic)
    
        
            Say you just landed from Mars and walked straight into the 2026 Whitney Biennial. Would you be able to tell from the show that the country is teetering on the precipice of fascism? That two American citizens have been shot dead in broad daylight by masked federal agents? That hard-working immigrants are being rounded up in Home Depot and Walmart stores and sent to concentration camps? That the current government unabashedly kidnaps and assassinates foreign leaders and lays claim to their countriesÔÇÖ oil?How about the fact that artists have been silenced and denied opportunities for their political views, including at the Whitney itself, where a program was put on hold and its leader sacked over a cohortÔÇÖs sympathy for Palestinians?┬áWould any of that come to mind at the storied exhibition this year? Not so much.┬áAnd yet, youÔÇÖre likely to walk out of the show feeling that somethingÔÇÖs rotten in the United States. Fear and inhibition are humming like drones beneath the surface. Otherwise, why so somber and moody? Why so meek and joyless?┬á┬á┬á┬á┬áBarring a few exceptions ÔÇö Ali EyalÔÇÖs Ferris wheel of horrors from his war-torn childhood in Baghdad; Kainoa GruspeÔÇÖs doorstops, made from found material in US military bases and golf courses in Hawaii ÔÇö I got the sense that the Whitney Biennial is hiding from the world today instead of reflecting on it.┬áAli Eyal, ÔÇťLook Where I Took YouÔÇŁ (2026) (photo Lakshmi Rivera Amin/Hyperallergic)This biennial is neither bad, nor ÔÇťsafe,ÔÇŁ nor ÔÇťweird." ItÔÇÖs not even apolitical ÔÇö it couldn't be, even if it tried. ItÔÇÖs just frightened.┬áItÔÇÖs a traumatized, faint-hearted biennial where artists mourn, commiserate, and snuggle together in ambient sound baths (Oswaldo Mac├şa, Young Joon Kwak), altars (Zach Blas), and shrines (Enzo Camacho and Ami Lien) while TrumpÔÇÖs Epic Fury rages outside.┬áEven while it avoids addressing the current political moment, the show makes sure to tick many seemingly progressive boxes. ItÔÇÖs more inclusive than ever, widening the tent of American art to embrace artists hailing from Palestine, Iraq, Vietnam, Japan, and the Philippines ÔÇö in other words, places screwed by American imperialism. It turns an analytical gaze onto systems of surveillance, extractivism, and oppression, as well as the infrastructures that sustain them. But all of this critical fluency  ÔÇö none of it groundbreaking ÔÇö fails to make a collective statement about the mess weÔÇÖre in. WerenÔÇÖt the Soviet gulags and Chinese re-education camps full of people well informed about the systems that oppressed them?┬áZach Blas, CULTUS (2023) (photo Hakim Bishara/Hyperallergic) ÔÇťTo some, the Biennial may not be ÔÇśpolitical enoughÔÇÖ if it fails to confront global leaders or conflicts,ÔÇŁ writes Whitney Museum Director Scott Rothkopf in his foreword to this biennialÔÇÖs catalog, in what may be an attempt to get ahead of the criticism. He goes on to explain that he personally prefers ÔÇťother forms of both art and political agencyÔÇŁ that are less confrontational, citing free admission programs (free Friday nights, free second Sundays, and free for visitors 25 and under) as examples of his political work. Marcela Guerrero, who co-curated the show with Drew Sawyer, writes in the same catalog that ÔÇťto insist that the arrival of the 2026 Biennial coincides with an especially urgent moment in history feels trite, self-indulgent, even.ÔÇŁ┬áThese rhetorical backflips cover up a fear of taking a stand in this fraught moment of American history, lest it upset a museum trustee or donor. This trepidation is also reflected in the choice to go themeless this year. In a press conference before the show, Guerrero explained that she and Sawyer ÔÇťdidnÔÇÖt want to go with a predetermined idea,ÔÇŁ and preferred to ÔÇťkeep an open mind,ÔÇŁ ultimately opting for moods and ÔÇťminor feelings.ÔÇŁ (Journalists were not allowed to ask questions, by the way.) I would never advocate for too-literal art, but a theme is how a group of works coalesces into a vision, a statement, or a historical record. Even the Museum of Modern Art, whose board is teeming with questionable billionaires, stood up to Trump in 2017 when it installed an impromptu protest show of artists from countries included in the first ÔÇťMuslim ban.ÔÇŁ Eschewing a meaningful theme in a time like this is equivalent to burying your head in the sand. The result is communion without a common cause. ÔÇťRelationalityÔÇŁ ÔÇö whatever the hell that is ÔÇö without solidarity. ┬áThe best and most memorable Whitney Biennials were the ones that took chances and werenÔÇÖt afraid to make mistakes. Amid protests against Vice-Chair Warren KandersÔÇÖs involvement in the manufacturing of tear gas used against asylum seekers and protesters, the curators of the 2019 biennial had the guts to include an investigation by the group Forensic Architecture into his dubious businesses. And whatever you thought of the choice to include Dana SchutzÔÇÖs controversial painting of Emmet Till in the 2017 edition, it prompted meaningful conversations about race, representation, and ethics in art. Decades from now, when weÔÇÖre living in Elon Musk-made, Palantir-surveilled condos on Mars, will anyone remember the 2026 Whitney Biennial?┬á

schema.headline: The Whitney Biennial Is for the Faint-Hearted
